item.title: About
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to Inside Korea.
This space is created to show you what Korea really looks and feels like —
beyond K-dramas, glossy travel guides, and trending hashtags.

I’m a Korean local who loves to explore small alleys, hidden cafés, markets,
and all the little details that make daily life here unique.
Through this blog, I share stories, photos, and local insights
to help you experience the real side of Korea —
not just as a tourist, but as someone living and breathing it.

Here, you’ll find:
• 🧳 Travel guides that go beyond typical attractions.
• ☕ Hidden cafés and food spots where locals actually go.
• 🎬 K-culture insights — how K-dramas, music, and fashion blend into real life.
• 💡 Practical tips for anyone visiting or planning to stay longer in Korea.

My goal is simple:
to make you feel like you’re walking the streets of Seoul,
sharing a coffee in Hongdae, or catching the sunset over the Han River.

Category

New Posts

item.title: Travel in Korea
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Korea is a country where tradition and technology meet — a place where ancient palaces stand beside skyscrapers, and mountain trails lead straight to vibrant city streets. Through Inside Korea Now, discover travel destinations that reveal the heart of modern Korea.

🏙️ City Adventures

From the neon streets of Seoul to the coastal charm of Busan, every city in Korea tells a different story. Each district offers a new mix of culture, food, and art waiting to be explored.
  • Seoul: A city that never sleeps, blending modern culture, shopping, and history at every corner.
  • Busan: Korea’s seaside capital, home to beaches, seafood markets, and ocean-view cafés.
  • Jeju Island: A volcanic paradise perfect for scenic drives, beaches, and healing stays.

🌿 Nature & Outdoor Experiences

Beyond the cities, Korea offers breathtaking nature — from coastal cliffs to mountain temples. Whether you prefer quiet hiking trails or seaside sunsets, there’s always something to explore.
  • National Parks: Visit Seoraksan, Hallasan, or Jirisan for panoramic views and clean air.
  • Temple Stays: Experience a peaceful night in Korea’s historic temples and learn Buddhist meditation.
  • Healing Spots: Discover hot springs, botanical gardens, and slow-travel villages designed for relaxation.

🍜 Local Culture on the Road

Every region in Korea has its own flavor — from spicy Jeonju bibimbap to fresh seafood in Busan. Traveling isn’t just about moving; it’s about tasting and living like a local.
  • Traditional Markets: Try street food and local snacks in Gwangjang or Dongmun Market.
  • Local Cafés: Discover independent cafés hidden in small alleys across Korea’s cities.
  • Festivals: Join seasonal celebrations that combine art, music, and community.

🚆 Travel Tips & Guides

Getting around Korea is easier than ever — thanks to its efficient transport system and traveler-friendly apps.
  • Transportation: Use KTX high-speed trains, buses, or rental cars for long-distance travel.
  • Public Transit: Korea’s metro systems in Seoul, Busan, and Daegu are among the world’s best.
  • Digital Tools: Download apps like Naver Map, Kakao T, and Subway Korea to make travel seamless.

🌏 Travel with Inside Korea Now

At Inside Korea Now, we share local tips, hidden routes, and real experiences from people who know the country best. Whether you’re a first-time visitor or a returning traveler, we’ll help you see Korea — not just as a tourist, but as a local explorer. Plan smart, travel slow, and experience the rhythm of Korea your own way.
Explore more: K-Culture | K-Food | Living in Korea
item.excerpt:encoded: Explore Korea beyond the map — from the neon streets of Seoul and beaches of Busan to the volcanic landscapes of Jeju. Discover local experiences, cultural travel tips, and hidden destinations across the country.

item.title: 4-Day Jeju Island Guide for First-Time Travelers (Where to Stay & Eat)
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
4-Day Jeju Island Guide for First-Time Travelers (Where to Stay & Eat)

Jeju Island is often described as Korea’s “Hawaii” — a stunning blend of volcanic landscapes, sea-side cafés, and local cuisine. This 4-day guide shows you where to stay, what to eat, and how to experience Jeju like a local.

Where to Stay

Jeju City (North): Ideal for arrivals and first-time travelers. Easy access to Dongmun Market and Black Pork Street.

Seogwipo (South): Perfect for slower travel — waterfalls, cliffs, and scenic drives await.

Aewol & Seongsan: Great for café lovers and ocean views, especially if you rent a car.

  • Short trips (1–2 days): Stay in Jeju City for convenience.
  • Longer stays (3+ days): Split between Jeju City and Seogwipo.
  • Using public transport: Stay near Jeju City center or bus terminals.

Where to Eat

Jeju’s local cuisine is one of the best parts of the island experience. Don’t leave without trying these:

  • Jeju Black Pork BBQ: Visit Donsadon or Hwaro Hyang along Black Pork Street in Jeju City.
  • Seafood: Try Gogi Guksu (pork noodle soup) or Galchi-jorim (braised beltfish) in Seogwipo.
  • Cafés: Bomnal Café and Paul & Mary in Aewol offer amazing ocean views and desserts.
  • Markets: Dongmun Traditional Market in Jeju City for snacks and souvenirs.

Food Tips: Have one meal for BBQ, one for seafood, and spend an afternoon in a scenic café. Bring a translation app for easy ordering!

4-Day Itinerary

Day 1 – Arrival & North Jeju

Arrive at Jeju Airport → Check-in at a Jeju City hotel → Explore Tapdong Plaza and Chilsung-ro → Lunch at Black Pork Street → Sunset at Hamdeok Beach → Night stroll through Dongmun Market.

Day 2 – East Jeju: Volcanic Landscapes

Sunrise at Seongsan Ilchulbong → Brunch near Sehwa Beach → Explore Manjanggul Cave → Dinner in Seogwipo at a local seafood spot.

Day 3 – South & West Jeju: Nature & Café Culture

Morning hike at Hallasan Mountain or visit Hyeopjae Beach → Lunch in Aewol café strip → Visit O’Sulloc Tea Museum → Sunset dinner at west coast seafood restaurant.

Day 4 – Relax & Departure

Café-hopping morning → Brunch and souvenir shopping (hallabong oranges, Jeju chocolates) → Return to Jeju City → Farewell meal → Fly out from Jeju Airport.

Traveler Tips

  • Rent a car if possible for full freedom.
  • Book accommodation near café streets for photos and convenience.
  • Include at least one “local experience” like a market visit or temple stay.
  • Save this post for your Jeju trip!

FAQ

Do I need a car to explore Jeju?
Public transport works, but a rental car gives freedom to visit hidden spots easily.

What food should I try in Jeju?
Jeju Black Pork BBQ and fresh seafood are essentials — try Donsadon or Seogwipo’s coastal restaurants.

Where’s the best place to stay?
Jeju City for convenience, Seogwipo for scenery, Aewol for ocean-view cafés.

Written by Inside Korea — Your local guide to authentic Korean travel and life.
item.excerpt:encoded: Planning your first trip to Jeju Island? This 4-day guide covers everything — from where to stay in Jeju City and Seogwipo to the best local food, cafés, and hidden beaches. Discover Jeju like a local with this easy itinerary and insider travel tips.

item.title: K-Pop Demon Hunters Festival Opens at Everland (2025 Autumn Special Event)
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
The K-Pop Demon Hunters Festival officially opened at Everland on September 26, 2025, and will run through late November. This large-scale collaboration brings K-pop fantasy to life with immersive games, character experiences, exclusive goods, and special performances.

📍 Event Dates & Location

The K-Pop Demon Hunters Theme Zone runs from September 26 to November 16, 2025, with ongoing stamp and roulette events. You can find the festival at Everland’s Festival Content Zone, located next to Space Tour.

🎮 Main Attractions

The Demon Hunters Theme Zone is divided into interactive experience zones, performance photo areas, and a limited-edition goods shop. Each section offers its own themed mini-games and mission-based challenges.

HuntRix Zone

  • “Crisis Flight” – A flight simulation shooting game that immerses players in fast-paced missions.
  • “Defend the Soul Gate” – A tap-based reaction game where players protect portals by hitting the right spots in time.
  • “Golden” Photo Zone – A performance-style photo booth that recreates the iconic Golden stage background.

Lion Boys Zone

  • “Complete the Song” – A rhythm game synchronized with the original OST, testing your timing and focus.
  • “Offer Your Soul” – A rolling ball challenge where players tilt the board to guide the ball to the target; the hardest of all four games.
  • “Your Idol” Photo Zone – A photo booth featuring the Lion Boys’ stage, complete with reaper hats and idol lighting effects.

🎭 Special Events & Programs

Stamp Mission

Complete all four mini-games to collect stamps. Once the mission is cleared, head to the Reward Zone to receive a random hologram card as a souvenir.

Costume & Makeup Experience

At the popup zone entrance, you can rent character costumes to transform into members of the Demon Hunters world. There’s also a professional makeup truck — the same used for artist tours — where visitors can get styled like Lumi or Jinwoo. The cost is approximately 20,000 KRW, and popular sizes sell out fast.

Limited Goods Shop

The merchandise shop offers 38 exclusive items such as keychains, magnets, cushions, and plush dolls — a must-visit for collectors.

🌟 Tips for Visitors

  • Go Early: Even on weekdays, queues can exceed an hour. Plan an “open-run” to experience popular zones first.
  • Complete Missions: Finishing all four games and collecting all stamps unlocks a unique Irwolobongdo-style hologram card.
  • Photo Spots: The “Golden” photo zone recreates a K-pop concert stage, while the “Your Idol” zone lets you pose as a reaper idol — perfect for Instagram photos.
  • Night Fireworks: After exploring the theme zone, don’t miss the Sing-Along Fireworks Show — a new evening performance that continues the Demon Hunters world from day to night.

🎆 A Day in the Demon Hunters World

During the day, experience thrilling missions and K-pop-inspired mini-games. As night falls, the park lights up for the K-Pop Demon Hunters Sing-Along Fireworks — completing the immersive “Demon Hunters World” experience at Everland.
Event Period: September 26 – November 30, 2025 Location: Everland Festival Content Zone, next to Space Tour, Yongin, South Korea

FAQ

Q: Do I need to reserve tickets in advance? A: Regular Everland admission includes access to the K-Pop Demon Hunters zone, but certain experiences may require on-site registration. Q: Are the games suitable for kids? A: Yes, most mini-games have easy modes, making them accessible for all ages. Q: Can I buy Demon Hunters goods online? A: Currently, official merchandise is available only at the on-site shop within Everland.
Written by Inside Korea — your local guide to Korea’s pop culture and travel.
item.excerpt:encoded: Everland’s K-Pop Demon Hunters Festival runs from September 26 to November 30, 2025 — featuring mission games, photo zones, costume experiences, and a new sing-along fireworks show that turns the park into a full K-pop fantasy world.

item.title: K-Food
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Korean food — or K-Food — is more than a meal; it’s a story of flavor, history, and togetherness. From sizzling street food to Michelin-starred dining, every dish reflects Korea’s deep-rooted traditions and evolving creativity.

🍲 The Essence of Korean Cuisine

Korean cuisine is built around balance — spicy and sweet, hot and cold, tradition and innovation. It celebrates community and the idea that food connects people, whether at a family table or a late-night market stall.
  • Kimchi: The iconic fermented dish found in every Korean meal — spicy, tangy, and full of probiotics.
  • Bibimbap: A colorful rice bowl topped with vegetables, meat, and a fried egg — harmony in one bowl.
  • Samgyeopsal: Grilled pork belly served with lettuce wraps and sauces, perfect for sharing.

🍢 Street Food Adventures

Korean street food culture is famous for its variety and affordability. Every corner of Seoul, Busan, and Jeonju offers a different bite of daily life.
  • Tteokbokki: Spicy rice cakes in red chili sauce — Korea’s most beloved street snack.
  • Hotteok: Sweet pancakes filled with cinnamon and sugar, perfect for winter days.
  • Fish Cake Skewers (Eomuk): Warm broth and chewy fish cakes, a comforting favorite for locals.

🥢 Dining Culture & Etiquette

Eating in Korea is a social experience. Meals are shared, conversations flow, and side dishes (banchan) are refilled endlessly. Understanding these small rituals helps you appreciate Korean food on a deeper level.
  • Always wait for elders to start eating first.
  • Use chopsticks and spoons respectfully — never stick chopsticks upright in a bowl of rice.
  • Try everything! Korean hospitality often means tasting a bit of every dish on the table.

🏡 Regional Specialties

Every region of Korea has its own signature flavors — influenced by geography, climate, and history. Traveling through Korea is like tasting a new story in every city.
  • Jeonju: Home of bibimbap and traditional Korean dining experiences.
  • Busan: Famous for seafood, especially grilled eel and raw fish platters.
  • Jeju Island: Known for black pork BBQ and fresh abalone dishes.

🌶️ Modern K-Food Trends

As K-Culture spreads worldwide, so does K-Food. Fusion dishes, vegan Korean recipes, and creative café menus are introducing new generations to Korean flavors.
  • Korean Fried Chicken: Crispy perfection coated in sweet or spicy sauces.
  • Dalgona Coffee: The viral whipped coffee born in Busan during the pandemic — now a café staple.
  • K-Food Abroad: Korean BBQ restaurants and kimchi festivals are growing in cities across the world.

🍚 Experience K-Food with Inside Korea Now

Whether you’re visiting Korea for the first time or exploring from home, Inside Korea Now helps you discover the country through its flavors — from traditional hanjeongsik to street snacks and café desserts. Eat, share, and connect — that’s the spirit of K-Food.
Explore more: Travel in Korea | K-Culture | Living in Korea

item.excerpt:encoded: Discover the flavors of K-Food — from sizzling street snacks to traditional Korean dining. Explore the culture, stories, and regional specialties that make Korean cuisine one of the world’s most beloved.

item.title: Living in Korea
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Living in Korea means experiencing a unique balance between tradition and modern life. From high-tech convenience and rich cultural heritage to a deep sense of community, Korea offers one of the most fascinating lifestyles in Asia. Whether you’re a student, expat, or digital nomad, here’s what life in Korea truly feels like.

🏙️ Daily Life in Korea

Life in Korea moves fast — but efficiently. Cities like Seoul and Busan are filled with 24-hour cafés, convenience stores, and cutting-edge public transport that make daily routines seamless.
  • Transportation: Subways, buses, and taxis run with precision, and most payments are made using T-money cards or mobile apps.
  • Convenience Stores: From snacks to bill payments, 24-hour stores like GS25 or CU are part of daily life.
  • Work & Study Culture: Productivity and education are key values — but cafés and parks provide balance and rest.

🏠 Housing & Cost of Living

Finding a place to live in Korea can be both exciting and challenging. Housing types vary from one-room studios and officetels to shared houses and apartments.
  • Monthly Rent: Expect around 500,000–1,000,000 KRW for a studio apartment in Seoul.
  • Jeonse System: A unique long-term deposit system that allows residents to live rent-free after paying a large security deposit.
  • Utilities: Korea’s heating (ondol) and air-conditioning systems are efficient, but energy bills rise in winter and summer.

🍜 Food & Lifestyle

Eating out is a big part of Korean life — and it’s affordable. From late-night BBQ to cozy cafés, food connects people and creates community everywhere.
  • Eating Out: Most locals dine out several times a week. A meal typically costs 7,000–12,000 KRW.
  • Café Culture: Korean cafés double as workspaces, social hubs, and creative studios.
  • Health & Wellness: Hiking, skincare, and spa culture (jjimjilbang) are essential parts of Korean life.

💬 Language & Communication

While English is widely used in cities, learning a bit of Korean can greatly enhance your experience. Simple greetings and polite expressions make daily interactions smoother — and locals appreciate the effort.
  • Apps to Learn: Try Duolingo, Talk To Me In Korean, or Naver Dictionary for easy study.
  • Everyday Words: “Annyeonghaseyo” (Hello), “Gamsahamnida” (Thank you), “Jal jinaesseyo?” (How have you been?)
  • Community: Join local expat groups or language exchanges to build friendships faster.

🌏 Living as an Expat or Digital Nomad

Korea’s fast internet, safe environment, and strong infrastructure make it an ideal place for remote work or study. Digital nomads love the balance between connectivity and creativity that Korean cities offer.
  • Visas: Check options like the H-1 Working Holiday Visa or D-2 Student Visa.
  • Wi-Fi Everywhere: Free public Wi-Fi is available in subways, cafés, and libraries.
  • Work-Life Balance: Korean workplaces are evolving, with flexible schedules and coworking spaces becoming popular.

🎌 Festivals, Culture & Community

Even everyday life in Korea is full of culture — from seasonal festivals to small neighborhood markets. It’s easy to feel part of something vibrant, no matter where you live.
  • Join traditional holidays like Chuseok (harvest festival) and Seollal (Lunar New Year).
  • Explore local art markets, indie concerts, and university street events.
  • Volunteer or join local clubs to connect with both Koreans and expats.

🧭 Living in Korea with Inside Korea Now

At Inside Korea Now, we share local insights to help you adapt smoothly — from finding your first apartment to discovering weekend getaways. Korea isn’t just a place to visit; it’s a lifestyle to experience and enjoy. Live well, eat well, and grow together — that’s life in Korea.
Explore more: Travel in
item.excerpt:encoded: Discover what everyday life in Korea feels like — from housing and food culture to work, study, and digital nomad life. Explore local insights and lifestyle tips to truly live like a local in Korea.

item.title: Explore Korea
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to Inside Korea Now — your complete guide to exploring, tasting, and living in Korea.
Whether you’re planning a trip, discovering K-culture, or dreaming of life in Seoul, this hub connects you to every part of the Korean experience.

🇰🇷 What You’ll Discover

Explore Korea through four unique journeys — from delicious flavors and iconic pop culture to travel adventures and everyday life insights.

🍱 K-Food: Taste the Culture

From sizzling street food to fine dining, Korean cuisine is full of flavor and story.
Learn where to eat, what to try, and how food connects people across the country.

🎵 K-Culture: Feel the Rhythm

Experience the heartbeat of modern Korea — K-pop, K-dramas, festivals, fashion, and art.
See how Korea’s creative wave continues to inspire the world.

✈️ Travel: Discover Korea Beyond the Map

Travel through Korea’s cities, coasts, and mountain trails.
Find hidden spots, local cafes, and weekend destinations that show you the real Korea.

🏙 Living in Korea: Life, Work, and Culture

What’s it like to live in Korea?
From housing and cost of living to digital nomad life, explore how locals and expats build their daily rhythm here

📰 Latest Stories

Inside Korea Now helps you explore the country with real stories, honest guides, and local insight — so you can experience Korea, your way.

item.title: K-Culture
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Korean culture — or K-Culture — has become one of the most dynamic and influential movements in the world. From K-pop and K-dramas to fashion, festivals, and local traditions, it’s a cultural wave that connects the past with the present and reaches every corner of the globe.

🎵 K-Pop and the Global Stage

The rise of K-pop has turned Seoul into a global cultural hub. Fans from all over the world visit Korea to attend live concerts, music shows, and fan events. Beyond entertainment, K-pop has become a shared language that transcends borders, bringing together people through rhythm, performance, and creativity.

  • Visit iconic K-pop landmarks like HYBE Insight or SM Town for a true fan experience.
  • Attend live tapings of music programs such as Inkigayo or Music Bank.
  • Explore pop-up exhibitions, album cafés, and special fan-themed events in Seoul.

🎭 K-Dramas, Fashion, and Lifestyle

K-dramas have introduced global audiences to Korean storytelling and everyday life. The unique blend of modern design and traditional values now defines Korean lifestyle culture — influencing fashion, beauty, and even architecture.

  • Walk through filming locations in Seoul, Busan, and Jeju featured in your favorite dramas.
  • Discover Korean fashion brands that blend streetwear and hanbok-inspired design.
  • Experience Korea’s beauty culture with skincare tours and local cosmetic stores.

🎉 Festivals and Cultural Experiences

Throughout the year, Korea hosts cultural festivals that celebrate music, food, art, and tradition. Whether you join the Boryeong Mud Festival, Seoul Lantern Festival, or Gwangju Biennale, you’ll experience how creativity and community come together.

  • Traditional meets modern: Explore events where heritage meets innovation — from temple stays to techno nights.
  • Interactive zones: Many festivals now include hands-on programs for international visitors.
  • Local connections: Talk with artists, volunteers, and locals who make each event memorable.

🌏 Why K-Culture Matters

K-Culture isn’t just about trends — it’s about how Korea shares its creativity with the world. Each song, film, or design carries a piece of history, modernity, and identity that continues to inspire millions.

Through Inside Korea Now, you can explore the energy of K-Culture up close — the sounds, sights, and stories that define a new global generation.

Explore more:
Travel in Korea | K-Food | Living in Korea
item.excerpt:encoded: Discover the energy of K-Culture — from K-Pop and K-Dramas to festivals, fashion, and lifestyle. Explore how modern Korea connects tradition and creativity to shape one of the world’s most influential cultural movements.

item.title: Top 10 Korean Street Foods You Must Try in Seoul
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Discover the Flavors of Seoul’s Street Food Culture

Seoul is a paradise for food lovers, offering a vibrant mix of flavors, textures, and aromas that reflect Korea’s dynamic culture. From sweet and spicy treats to savory bites, Korean street food is not just a meal — it’s an experience. Here are the top 10 must-try street foods you should not miss when visiting Seoul.

1. Tteokbokki (Spicy Rice Cakes)

Soft rice cakes drenched in a sweet and spicy gochujang (red chili paste) sauce — tteokbokki is the ultimate comfort food. You’ll find it in almost every market, especially at Gwangjang Market and Myeongdong.

2. Hotteok (Sweet Korean Pancake)

This warm, chewy pancake filled with brown sugar, honey, cinnamon, and nuts is a winter favorite. The crispy outside and gooey inside make it irresistible, especially from vendors in Insadong and Namdaemun Market.

3. Odeng (Fish Cake Skewers)

Served on skewers in a savory broth, odeng is a quick and satisfying snack. It’s perfect for chilly evenings, often enjoyed with a cup of the warm soup the skewers are cooked in.

4. Gimbap (Seaweed Rice Rolls)

Often called the Korean version of sushi, gimbap is made with rice, vegetables, egg, and sometimes meat, all rolled in seaweed. It’s portable, filling, and perfect for travelers on the go.

5. Twigim (Korean-Style Tempura)

Korean twigim includes deep-fried vegetables, squid, sweet potatoes, and more. Dip them into tteokbokki sauce for an authentic street food combo that locals love.

6. Sundae (Korean Blood Sausage)

Made from glass noodles and pig’s blood, sundae may sound intimidating, but it’s a beloved classic. Try it with a sprinkle of salt and chili powder at Gwangjang Market.

7. Dakkochi (Grilled Chicken Skewers)

Juicy, grilled chicken glazed with spicy or sweet soy sauce — dakkochi is smoky, flavorful, and satisfying. The aroma alone will make you stop at any street cart serving it.

8. Bungeoppang (Fish-Shaped Pastry)

This cute fish-shaped pastry is filled with sweet red bean paste, custard, or even chocolate. It’s crispy on the outside and soft inside — a perfect snack while strolling Seoul’s streets in winter.

9. Tornado Potato (Hweori Gamja)

A whole potato cut into a spiral and deep-fried on a stick, then sprinkled with cheese or chili powder. It’s crunchy, fun to eat, and Instagram-worthy!

10. Japchae Hot Dog

This modern twist combines Korean glass noodles (japchae) with a deep-fried hot dog — a delicious fusion of East and West. You can find it at trendy food trucks in Hongdae or Dongdaemun.

Where to Find the Best Street Food in Seoul

Some of the best street food hubs include Myeongdong Night Market, Gwangjang Market, Namdaemun Market, and Hongdae Food Street. Each offers a different vibe — from traditional flavors to trendy innovations.

Final Thoughts

Exploring Seoul’s street food scene is more than just eating — it’s a cultural journey through Korea’s rich history and culinary creativity. Whether you’re a foodie or a casual traveler, these top 10 street foods will leave you craving for more.
item.excerpt:encoded: Explore the top 10 Korean street foods you must try in Seoul — from spicy tteokbokki and sweet hotteok to crispy twigim and fish-shaped pastries. A must-read guide for every foodie visiting Korea!

item.title: Top 5 Tteokbokki Restaurants in Seoul You Must Try for Authentic Korean Flavor
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Experience the Best Tteokbokki in Seoul

When it comes to Korean street food, nothing beats a warm plate of tteokbokki — chewy rice cakes drenched in sweet and spicy gochujang sauce. In Seoul, you’ll find countless spots serving their own twist on this beloved dish. Whether you prefer the classic retro flavor or a modern spicy version, here are five tteokbokki restaurants in Seoul worth visiting.

1. Cheolgil Tteokbokki – Chungjeongno

Cheolgil Tteokbokki (철길떡볶이) in Chungjeongno is one of Seoul’s most nostalgic spots for tteokbokki lovers. As its name suggests (“railway tteokbokki”), it’s located near the train tracks and subway station, making it a convenient stop for both locals and travelers. The spicy yet slightly sweet sauce captures the true essence of traditional Seoul-style tteokbokki.

📍 Location:

Near Chungjeongno Station, Jung-gu, Seoul

⭐ Highlight:

Retro flavors, nostalgic vibe, and easy access from the subway

2. Byun Gang-soe Tteokbokki – Apgujeong

Byun Gang-soe Tteokbokki (변강쇠떡볶이) in Apgujeong is a celebrity favorite. Known as one of Seoul’s iconic “old-school” tteokbokki joints, it offers bold, spicy flavors that blend sweetness and heat perfectly. Even Korean entertainers have been spotted here, making it a famous spot among locals and visitors alike.

📍 Location:

Apgujeong, Gangnam-gu, Seoul

⭐ Highlight:

Celebrity-approved flavor and nostalgic Seoul-style sauce

3. Mapo Original Tteokbokki – Dohwa-dong

Mapo Original Tteokbokki (마포원조떡볶이) is a legendary tteokbokki house located at 3 Dohwa 2-gil, Mapo-gu. It operates daily from 8:30 a.m. to 9:00 p.m., serving rich and flavorful tteokbokki that hasn’t changed for decades. Be sure not to confuse it with similarly named stores — this is the authentic one cherished by locals.

📍 Location:

3 Dohwa 2-gil, Mapo-gu, Seoul

⭐ Highlight:

Authentic Mapo flavor, long history, and consistent taste

4. Pungnyeon Ssal Nongsan – Samcheong-dong

Pungnyeon Ssal Nongsan (풍년쌀농산) in Samcheong-dong stands out for its unique take on rice-based tteokbokki. Using fresh rice cakes made from premium grains, the texture is softer and lighter than typical street tteokbokki. It’s a great spot for those who want to enjoy a more delicate and refined version of this Korean classic.

📍 Location:

Samcheong-dong, Jongno-gu, Seoul

⭐ Highlight:

Unique rice-based tteokbokki and traditional Korean atmosphere

5. Apple House – Isu Station

Apple House (애플하우스) near Isu Station is one of Seoul’s top three legendary tteokbokki spots. It’s famously known as actor Jo In-sung’s go-to snack place. The combination of spicy-sweet tteokbokki, fried snacks, and the warm, cozy atmosphere makes it a must-visit for anyone exploring Seoul’s comfort food scene.

📍 Location:

Near Isu Station, Dongjak-gu, Seoul

⭐ Highlight:

Celebrity favorite, one of Seoul’s top 3 tteokbokki spots, near the subway

Final Thoughts

Each of these five spots offers a different take on Seoul’s favorite comfort food. From nostalgic flavors to modern spicy kicks, tteokbokki in Seoul is more than just a snack — it’s a piece of local history. Whether you’re a foodie or a casual traveler, make sure to add these restaurants to your must-try list.
item.excerpt:encoded: Discover the top 5 tteokbokki restaurants in Seoul offering everything from nostalgic railway-style flavors to celebrity-approved hot spots. Perfect for anyone who loves authentic Korean street food!

item.title: Top Street Foods to Try Near Hongdae Station: From Tteokbokki to 35cm Skewers
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Discover the Best Street Foods in Hongdae

Hongdae is one of Seoul’s liveliest neighborhoods — a fusion of music, youth, and street food. Just a few steps from Hongdae Station, you’ll find endless food stalls offering everything from crispy snacks to sweet desserts. Let’s explore the most popular street foods and must-visit spots in this trendy area!

🍡 Hongdae’s Most Popular Street Foods

From savory skewers to sweet pastries, Hongdae’s street food scene never disappoints. Here are some of the top picks loved by locals and tourists alike.

🍢 Dakkochi (Grilled Chicken Skewers)

Ppilkkkochine Hongdae (삘꼬치네 홍대) is famous for its juicy grilled chicken skewers. The smoky aroma and spicy glaze make it one of Hongdae’s all-time favorite street snacks — especially popular during cool evenings when the streets come alive with music and lights.

🍞 Sweet & Savory Breads

  • Seoul Hotteok (서울호떡) – A must-try sweet pancake filled with brown sugar and nuts, perfect for a cozy snack.
  • 10 Won Bread (이쩜오 홍대점) – This trendy mini bread is soft, fluffy, and filled with sweet cream. It’s gone viral on social media for its cute coin-like shape.
  • Croissant Bungeoppang – A modern twist on Korea’s classic fish-shaped pastry, this version uses croissant dough for an extra crispy texture. Popular in both Hongdae and Myeongdong.

🍥 Other Local Favorites

  • Moon Skewers (문꼬치) – A fun, chewy treat grilled to perfection.
  • Tteokgalbi Meatballs (떡갈비 완자) – Juicy Korean meatballs served on sticks; you can also find them in Myeongdong.
  • Fried Banana (바나나 튀김) – Sweet and crispy, a favorite dessert option after a night out in Hongdae.
  • Tteokbokki (떡볶이) – Korea’s ultimate street food, made with rice cakes simmered in spicy gochujang sauce, often served with fish cakes and boiled eggs.
  • Crepes & Pancakes – PINKS Crepe Pancake Hongdae and Dalkkot Crepe (달꽃크레페 홍대2호점) are perfect for dessert lovers. Try their Nutella Banana Crepe — a favorite in both Hongdae and Myeongdong.

📍 Must-Visit Street Food Spots Near Hongdae Station

For an unforgettable foodie experience, don’t miss these popular locations near Hongdae Entrance Station:

  • K1 35cm Hongdae – Famous for its giant 35cm-long skewers filled with meat, cheese, and vegetables.
  • Luna Pie Hongdae – Offers warm, freshly baked mini pies in various flavors — from apple cinnamon to chocolate cream.
  • Wangga Tanghulu Hongdae – Known for shiny, crystal-coated fruit skewers that are as pretty as they are delicious.
  • 1004 Donut Hongdae – A must-stop for dessert lovers, featuring freshly fried donuts and seasonal toppings.

💡 Final Tips for Food Lovers

Hongdae’s street food scene captures the youthful, creative energy of Seoul. Visit in the evening for the best atmosphere, when lights glow and food stalls line the streets. Whether you’re craving spicy, sweet, or savory, Hongdae has something for every taste bud.
item.excerpt:encoded: Explore the best Hongdae street foods near Hongdae Station — from 35cm skewers and sweet hotteok to croissant bungeoppang and tanghulu. A must-read Seoul food guide for travelers and locals alike!

item.title: Best Korean BBQ Restaurants in Seoul Loved by Locals and Michelin Guide
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
🔥 Discover the Ultimate Korean BBQ Experience in Seoul

Seoul is home to some of the best Korean BBQ restaurants in the world — from Michelin-recognized steakhouses to cozy local joints beloved by celebrities. For foreign visitors, Korean BBQ offers more than just grilled meat — it’s an immersive dining culture that blends smoky aromas, shared dishes, and lively atmosphere. Here’s your curated guide to the top Korean BBQ spots in Seoul that will make your trip unforgettable.

🍖 Michelin Guide-Recommended Korean BBQ

These restaurants are praised by the Michelin Guide for their top-quality meats, expert grilling, and authentic Korean flavors.

🥩 Premium Beef Restaurants

  • Beodeunamu House (버드나무집) – Since 1977, this legendary restaurant has specialized in 1++ grade Korean beef (Hanwoo). The marinated galbi and jumulleok (lightly seasoned beef) are grilled over charcoal for a smoky finish. Their signature salt-seasoned beef ribs — with no soy sauce — highlight the meat’s pure, buttery flavor.
  • Gombawi (곰바위) – Serving premium cuts like yang (tripe) and daechang (beef intestines) for over 30 years, this Samsung-dong gem is famous for its Baekyang-gui. From ribeye to brisket, every cut is melt-in-your-mouth perfection.
  • Gukbinkan Tonguidong (통의동 국빈관) – Set in a traditional hanok house, this restaurant offers a peaceful dining experience with Hanwoo T-bone steak and charcoal-grilled marinated ribs. Enjoy your meal while admiring the serene courtyard views.

🐔 Chicken BBQ

  • Semigye (세미계) – Located in Hannam-dong, this charcoal-grilled chicken restaurant offers tender, lightly pre-grilled chicken paired with cheese fondue and tortilla wraps. Try the rare cuts like chicken neck or thigh for a gourmet twist on Korea’s beloved dakgalbi.

🐷 Pork BBQ

  • Geum Dwaeji Sikdang (금돼지식당) – Situated in Sindang-dong, this stylish spot with white tiles and gold signage draws long lines daily. It serves YBD pork (Yorkshire, Berkshire, Duroc crossbreed) for premium texture and juiciness. The bone-in samgyeopsal and neck cuts grilled on hot charcoal are simply divine.

🐑 Lamb BBQ

  • Ichiryu (이치류) – Korea’s first Sapporo-style lamb BBQ restaurant, known for using only fresh Australian lamb under one year old. The staff grills the meat tableside, making it an approachable choice even for first-time lamb eaters.

🐙 Seafood BBQ

  • Chungmuro Jjukkumi Bulgogi (충무로 쭈꾸미 불고기) – Since 1976, this spot has specialized in charcoal-grilled spicy baby octopus. The signature sauce — called the “magic sauce” — is addictively savory and spicy. The assorted combo with scallops and shellfish is a must-try.

🌟 More Korean BBQ Spots Loved by Locals

📍 Hongdae Area

  • Cheonggiwa Galbi (청기와갈비) – Just two minutes from Hongdae Station Exit 1, this long-standing BBQ restaurant offers valet parking and a refined interior. Their Hanwoo short ribs are best enjoyed lightly grilled with sea salt — a true melt-in-your-mouth experience.
  • Don Blanc (돈블랑) – A popular Hongdae pork BBQ spot loved by locals and tourists alike, known for its thick-cut samgyeopsal and modern dining vibe.
  • Wonjo Sutbul Sogeumgui (원조 숯불소금구이) – Located near Hwayang Intersection, this retro-style restaurant offers a cozy atmosphere with authentic charcoal-grilled meats that capture the essence of traditional Korean BBQ.

📍 Other Popular Districts

  • Choego Galbi (최고갈비 등촌역본점) – A favorite among returning tourists who crave authentic Korean marinated ribs.
  • Montbri (몽블리 명동) – Famous for its all-you-can-eat beef and pork BBQ infused with rich charcoal aroma — the perfect dinner spot after shopping in Myeongdong.
  • Mapo Jip (마포집 강남) – A true “local’s favorite,” known for its pork ribs and lively after-work atmosphere. Expect an authentic Korean dining scene filled with chatter and soju glasses clinking.

💡 Korean BBQ Tips for Visitors

When enjoying Korean BBQ, it’s not just about the meat — the side dishes, lettuce wraps, and sauces complete the experience. Pair each bite with fresh garlic, ssamjang, and kimchi. To finish your meal like a local, order a bowl of doenjang jjigae (soybean stew) or cold naengmyeon noodles.

Korean BBQ in Seoul isn’t just a meal — it’s a celebration of flavor, culture, and togetherness.
item.excerpt:encoded: Explore the best Korean BBQ restaurants in Seoul — from Michelin-starred Hanwoo beef houses to trendy Hongdae and Myeongdong spots. A complete guide for visitors seeking authentic Korean grilling culture.

item.title: Best Cafes in Insadong Seoul: Top Picks for 2026
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
Why Insadong Is Seoul's Most Charming Café Destination

Tucked between the glass towers of modern Seoul, Insadong remains one of the city's most beloved and culturally rich neighborhoods. Winding alleyways lined with traditional tea houses, independent galleries, and antique shops give this district a distinctly old-Seoul atmosphere that feels refreshingly different from the trendy café streets of Hongdae or Gangnam. For visitors in 2026, Insadong's café scene continues to evolve — blending deep-rooted Korean tea culture with creative, Instagram-worthy concepts that keep travelers coming back. Whether you're looking for a serene spot to sip omija (five-flavor berry) tea in a hanok courtyard, indulge in a sculptural bingsu dessert, or explore multi-story café complexes hiding boutiques and craft shops inside, Insadong delivers an experience that goes well beyond a simple cup of coffee. This guide covers the best cafés in Insadong Seoul for 2026, with practical tips to help you plan the perfect visit.

What Makes Insadong Cafés Unique

Cafés in Insadong aren't just about the drinks — they're about atmosphere, craft, and cultural storytelling. Many establishments here are housed in beautifully restored hanok (traditional Korean wooden houses), where wooden beams, courtyard gardens, and paper-screened windows create an ambiance you simply won't find anywhere else in the city. Others occupy quirky multilevel buildings filled with local art and handmade goods. The menu culture also differs here. While specialty espresso drinks are available, many Insadong cafés proudly champion traditional Korean beverages — think yujacha (citron tea), sikhye (sweet rice punch), sujeonggwa (cinnamon persimmon punch), and an array of floral and herbal teas. Desserts often incorporate ingredients like injeolmi (roasted rice cake), black sesame, red bean, and matcha, giving even the sweetest treats a distinctly Korean identity.

Best Cafés in Insadong: Our Top Picks for 2026

1. Ssamziegil Courtyard Cafés

Ssamziegil is less a single café and more a curated open-air complex that has become one of Insadong's most iconic destinations. The spiral walkway winds past independent stalls, craft vendors, and small café outlets selling everything from traditional grain lattes to hand-poured drip coffee. Visiting in 2026, you'll find the complex has refreshed several of its food and beverage tenants, with new seasonal menus that highlight local, sustainable ingredients. The best strategy here is to grab a dalgona candy pop or a warm cup of barley latte (bori latte) from one of the courtyard vendors and find a spot on the open terrace to people-watch. The atmosphere is especially magical in autumn when the surrounding ginkgo trees turn golden. Entry to Ssamziegil is free, making it an easy stop on any Insadong itinerary.

2. Dawon Tea House (다원)

For an authentic introduction to Korean tea culture, Dawon inside the Unhyeongung Palace complex is an unmissable stop. Set within a real historic hanok surrounded by a traditional garden, Dawon offers one of the most serene café experiences in all of Seoul. In 2026, it remains largely unchanged — and that's exactly the point. The menu focuses on traditional teas and tteok (rice cake) sets. Order the omija cha (五味茶) for a stunning ruby-red tea made from five-flavor berries, served in a ceramic cup with a small plate of seasonal rice cakes on the side. Seating is on low cushioned chairs around wooden tables in the courtyard garden. This is a cash-preferred location, so keep a few Korean won on hand.

3. Cha Masineun Ttul (차 마시는 뜰)

Translating roughly to "Garden for Drinking Tea," Cha Masineun Ttul is one of Insadong's longest-running and most beloved traditional tea houses. Hidden down a narrow alley off the main Insadong-gil street, this two-story café is built around an open inner courtyard filled with potted plants, stone lanterns, and the sound of trickling water. The menu spans over 30 varieties of Korean herbal teas, including chrysanthemum (gukhwa cha), ginger (saenggang cha), and jujube (daechu cha). For first-timers, the sampler set lets you try three different teas alongside a seasonal dessert. The upstairs seating area overlooks the courtyard and is particularly popular — arrive early on weekends to secure a window seat.

4. Insadong Teahouse (인사동 찻집)

A favorite among both locals and travelers, Insadong Teahouse strikes a lovely balance between traditional aesthetics and modern comfort. The interior features exposed brick, wooden furniture, and soft lighting that makes it equally welcoming on a rainy afternoon or a brisk winter morning. In 2026, their signature injeolmi latte — a creamy, lightly sweet drink dusted with roasted soybean powder — has become one of the most talked-about beverages in the neighborhood. They also do excellent patbingsu (shaved ice with red bean) in the warmer months, served in generous portions that are more than enough to share between two people. The staff are accustomed to international visitors and can explain each menu item in English, which is a genuine bonus in this part of Seoul.

5. Café Bora (카페 보라)

If you've spent any time browsing Korean travel content online, you've almost certainly seen Café Bora's striking purple soft-serve cones. Located just off the main Insadong strip near Anguk station, Bora is instantly recognizable for its dramatic use of purple taro and black sesame in everything from soft-serve ice cream to lattes and churros. The signature purple cone has become one of Seoul's most photographed food items, and for good reason — it tastes as good as it looks. Lines can still get long during peak hours, but in 2026 the café has expanded its seating and streamlined its ordering system, making the experience smoother than in previous years. Go on a weekday morning for the shortest waits. The Bora Latte — a lavender-hued drink combining taro and milk — is a gentler option if you prefer something to sip rather than lick.

6. Bukchon Teahouse (북촌 한옥 찻집)

Technically straddling the boundary between Insadong and Bukchon, this beautifully restored hanok teahouse is close enough to include as a natural extension of your Insadong café crawl. The building itself is a heritage-listed structure, and drinking tea inside feels like stepping back into Joseon-era Seoul. The menu is traditional and concise — a handful of seasonal teas, rice cakes, and sweet bean desserts. This is the kind of place where you come to slow down. There is no Wi-Fi, no background music beyond birdsong, and no pressure to order quickly. In a city that often moves at breakneck pace, that kind of intentional stillness is genuinely rare and refreshing.

7. Tezuka Coffee & Craft

For visitors who want excellent specialty coffee alongside a curated selection of Korean craft goods, Tezuka Coffee & Craft has become one of Insadong's most talked-about newcomers in 2026. The café doubles as a small gallery and concept store, with rotating exhibitions by emerging Korean artists displayed on whitewashed walls. The espresso program is serious — they source single-origin beans from Korean roasters and serve filter coffee using both Chemex and V60 pour-over methods. Their hojicha white chocolate latte has developed a cult following among regulars. If you're a coffee purist among a group of tea lovers, this is the café where everyone wins.

Practical Tips for Café-Hopping in Insadong

Getting There

Insadong is easily reached by Seoul Metro. Take Line 3 (Orange Line) to Anguk Station (Exit 6) and walk south for about five minutes along Insadong-gil. Alternatively, Jonggak Station on Line 1 provides access from the southern end of the neighborhood.

Best Time to Visit

Insadong is busiest on weekends and public holidays, when the main street becomes pedestrian-only and street performers add to the festive atmosphere. For a quieter café experience, visit on a weekday morning between 10am and noon. Autumn (October–November) is arguably the most beautiful season, with falling leaves framing the hanok rooftops perfectly.

Payment and Language Tips

  • Most cafés in Insadong accept major credit cards and T-Money, but a few traditional tea houses remain cash-only — carry some Korean won just in case.
  • Google Translate's camera feature works well for Korean menus, but many places in Insadong now offer English or picture menus for international visitors.
  • Naver Maps is more accurate than Google Maps for finding specific alley addresses in Insadong — worth downloading before your visit.

Café Crawl Itinerary Suggestion

  1. Start your morning at Tezuka Coffee & Craft for a specialty pour-over to kick things off.
  2. Walk to Cha Masineun Ttul for a traditional tea sampler and a quiet moment in the courtyard.
  3. Browse Ssamziegil complex over lunch and pick up a snack from one of the courtyard vendors.
  4. Stop at Café Bora in the early afternoon for the famous purple soft-serve.
  5. End the day with a serene sunset tea at Dawon inside the Unhyeongung Palace grounds.

Cultural Etiquette

In traditional tea houses, it's considered polite to speak quietly and take your time — these are spaces built for contemplation, not quick consumption. Avoid taking flash photography inside historic hanok interiors without asking first. Tipping is not customary in Korean cafés and may even cause mild confusion, so simply express your appreciation with a warm "gamsahamnida" (감사합니다 — thank you).

Conclusion: Insadong's Café Culture Is Worth Savoring Slowly

In a city renowned for its relentless pace and constant reinvention, Insadong's café scene stands out as a place where time slows down and culture takes center stage. Whether you're a seasoned Seoul traveler revisiting a neighborhood you love or a first-timer discovering traditional Korean tea culture for the very first time, the best cafés in Insadong offer something genuinely memorable in 2026 — beauty, flavor, history, and hospitality all poured into the same cup. Go with an open itinerary, follow your nose down the alleyways, and don't be surprised if a "quick coffee stop" turns into a two-hour afternoon of quiet discovery. That's the Insadong way.
Planning your Seoul café adventure in 2026? Save this guide to your travel board and share it with a friend who loves Korean culture — it might just inspire their next trip!

Frequently Asked Questions

Where exactly is Insadong located in Seoul?

Insadong is a traditional cultural district in central Seoul, situated between Gyeongbokgung Palace and Tapgol Park. The easiest way to get there is by subway — take Line 3 to Anguk Station (Exit 6) and walk south along the main Insadong-gil street. The neighborhood is compact and very walkable.

Are Insadong cafés expensive compared to other parts of Seoul?

Prices in Insadong are generally on par with other tourist-friendly neighborhoods in Seoul. A traditional tea set typically costs between ₩8,000–₩15,000 KRW, and specialty coffee drinks run ₩6,000–₩10,000 KRW. You may pay a slight premium at historic hanok venues for the atmosphere, but it's rarely excessive.

Do Insadong cafés have English menus?

Many of the more popular cafés and tea houses in Insadong provide English menus or picture menus, especially those that frequently host international visitors. Traditional tea houses may have Korean-only menus, but staff are generally patient and helpful. Downloading Google Translate or Papago for offline use is always a smart backup.

What traditional Korean drinks should I try in Insadong?

First-time visitors should try omija cha (five-flavor berry tea), yujacha (citron honey tea), and sikhye (sweet fermented rice punch). If you're curious about grain-based lattes, look for bori latte (barley latte) and injeolmi latte (roasted rice cake latte) — both are uniquely Korean and delicious.

Is Café Bora in Insadong still worth visiting in 2026?

Yes — Café Bora remains one of Seoul's most distinctive café experiences and continues to be popular in 2026. The purple taro soft-serve is genuinely tasty, not just photogenic. To avoid long lines, visit on a weekday morning or during off-peak hours in the early evening.

Are Insadong cafés suitable for families with children?

Absolutely. Many Insadong cafés are family-friendly, with dessert-focused menus that kids tend to enjoy — especially the bingsu, soft-serve, and rice cake sets. Ssamziegil in particular is a great family destination, with open courtyard spaces and a variety of food and craft stalls to explore together.

What is the best season to visit Insadong for cafés and atmosphere?

Autumn (late September through November) is widely considered the best time to visit Insadong, when the neighborhood's trees turn vivid shades of orange and gold and the cooler air makes walking between cafés especially pleasant. Spring (April–May) is a close second, with cherry blossoms adding to the charm. Summer can be hot and humid but the neighborhood remains lively.
item.excerpt:encoded: Insadong is one of Seoul's most charming neighborhoods for cafe hopping, blending traditional Korean culture with modern coffee culture. Here are the top cafes you can't miss in 2026.

item.title: SBS “Taxi Driver 3”: Why You Should Catch Up Before the Explosive New Season
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
🚖 The Return of Justice — Taxi Driver 3 Premieres This November

The beloved SBS drama series “Taxi Driver” is making its much-awaited comeback with Season 3, premiering on November 21, 2025. Following the success of its first two seasons, this action-packed revenge thriller returns to deliver more intense, emotional stories centered around justice for the powerless. If you haven’t watched Seasons 1 and 2 yet — now’s the perfect time to catch up!

🌆 A Story of Revenge, Justice, and Redemption

Based on the popular webtoon, Taxi Driver portrays the mysterious taxi company Rainbow Transport, which takes justice into its own hands when the law fails. Led by Kim Do-gi (played by Lee Je-hoon), the team avenges victims of injustice through daring, strategic missions that leave wrongdoers trembling. The show’s mix of crime, action, and moral storytelling has made it one of SBS’s most compelling dramas in recent years.

🔥 Season 1 — The Beginning of the Ride (2021)

  • Broadcast Period: April 9 – May 29, 2021
  • Starring: Lee Je-hoon, Esom
  • Production: Studio S & Group Eight
  • What Made It Special: Season 1 introduced audiences to the dark yet thrilling world of private revenge. Each episode tackled real-world issues — from bullying to fraud — giving viewers a sense of cathartic justice. Fans praised the drama’s sharp storytelling and emotional impact.

💬 Viewer Tip: Watch Season 1 to understand Kim Do-gi’s tragic past and how Rainbow Transport came together — it’s the emotional core of the entire series.

⚡ Season 2 — Bigger Missions, Deeper Emotions (2023)

  • Broadcast Period: February 17 – April 15, 2023
  • Genre: Crime · Action · Thriller (Rated 15+)
  • Highlights: Season 2 raised the stakes with global-scale missions, tighter team dynamics, and jaw-dropping twists. Critics called it “more polished and emotionally charged” than Season 1. Lee Je-hoon’s acting received rave reviews for balancing cold vengeance with human compassion.

💬 Viewer Tip: If you loved K-dramas like Vincenzo or The Glory, you’ll be hooked by Taxi Driver 2 — it combines moral dilemmas with stylish action.

🚨 Season 3 — The Most Anticipated Return of 2025

  • Premiere Date: November 21, 2025 (Fridays & Saturdays at 9:50 p.m. on SBS)
  • Episodes: 16 total
  • Genre: Action · Crime · Revenge · Social Commentary
  • Cast & Crew: Lee Je-hoon returns as Kim Do-gi, with Studio S, Group Eight, and BA Entertainment leading production. Directed by Kang Bo-seung and written by Oh Sang-ho, the season promises even more heart-pounding missions and moral questions about justice.

In Season 3, Kim Do-gi will face new enemies and even greater personal trials. The teaser hints at darker tones and higher emotional stakes, proving that this installment could be the most powerful yet.

🎬 Why You Should Watch “Taxi Driver” Before Season 3

Both Seasons 1 and 2 have been praised for their bold storytelling, emotional depth, and realistic portrayal of social injustices in Korea. The show’s ability to mix revenge, empathy, and redemption makes it a standout among modern K-dramas. Whether you’re a fan of thrillers or human dramas, Taxi Driver offers the perfect balance of excitement and meaning.

👉 Recommended Viewing Order: Start from Season 1 for the backstory, move to Season 2 for high-stakes action, and be ready for Season 3’s grand finale this November!

💡 Final Thoughts

Taxi Driver 3 is shaping up to be one of SBS’s biggest hits of 2025. It’s not just about revenge — it’s about hope, resilience, and fighting for what’s right. If you’re looking for a drama that thrills your heart and challenges your mind, Taxi Driver is a ride you don’t want to miss.
item.excerpt:encoded: SBS’s hit K-drama “Taxi Driver” returns with Season 3 on November 21, 2025. Catch up on the thrilling first two seasons before the ultimate revenge story continues with Lee Je-hoon’s powerful performance.

item.title: Cost of Living in Korea in 2025: My Honest Experience Living in Seoul
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
💰 Living in Korea 2025 — What It Really Costs

If you’ve ever dreamed of living in Korea — especially in Seoul — you’ve probably wondered how much it actually costs. After spending over a year here, I can say: Korea in 2025 isn’t cheap, but it’s not impossible either. From rent to coffee, here’s my honest review of what daily life costs in one of Asia’s most dynamic cities.

🏠 Housing Costs: Seoul’s Biggest Expense

Let’s be real — housing in Seoul is expensive. Most foreigners choose between a one-room officetel or a shared apartment. Here’s what I’ve personally paid and heard from other expats:

  • Officetel near Hongdae or Gangnam: ₩900,000–₩1,400,000 per month (USD 650–1,000)
  • Shared apartment (2~3 people): ₩500,000–₩800,000 per month (USD 360–600)
  • Deposit (jeonse/wolse): Most landlords require a ₩5–10 million deposit for short-term leases.

💬 Tip: If you’re staying long-term, consider using “Gosiwon” (a tiny studio room) — it’s small but affordable, starting around ₩400,000/month near universities.

🍜 Food & Dining: Eating Well Without Going Broke

One thing I love about Korea — you can eat delicious food at almost any price. From cheap convenience stores to cozy BBQ spots, there’s something for every budget.

  • Street food (tteokbokki, hotteok, etc.): ₩3,000–₩6,000 per item
  • Casual Korean meal (bibimbap, kimchi stew): ₩8,000–₩12,000
  • Korean BBQ with friends: ₩20,000–₩35,000 per person
  • Starbucks coffee: ₩5,500–₩7,000 (yes, it’s pricey)

Cooking at home is cheaper, but groceries aren’t exactly cheap. Imported food items can double your grocery bill. I usually spend about ₩300,000–₩400,000 a month on food when eating out a few times a week.

🚇 Transportation: Efficient and Affordable

Korea’s public transportation system is one of the best in the world. You can go anywhere in Seoul with a T-money card for just ₩1,400–₩1,700 per ride. The subway is clean, safe, and always on time.

  • Subway & Bus: Around ₩70,000/month for daily commuting
  • Taxi base fare: ₩4,800 (rising quickly with distance or late-night rides)
  • KTX (High-speed train): ₩60,000–₩70,000 for Seoul → Busan

💬 Pro Tip: Use apps like KakaoT or Naver Map — they’re lifesavers for getting around without confusion.

📱 Utilities & Daily Life

Internet speed in Korea is amazing — no surprise there! Most apartments include internet in their rent, but if not, it’s around ₩30,000/month. Phone plans can vary depending on your data needs:

  • Phone plan: ₩45,000–₩70,000/month (with unlimited data)
  • Utilities (electricity, gas, water): ₩80,000–₩150,000/month depending on season
  • Gym membership: ₩50,000–₩80,000/month

💬 My tip: winter heating can be expensive — expect your gas bill to double in January and February.

🎭 Entertainment & Lifestyle

Life in Seoul isn’t just about work. Koreans know how to have fun — from karaoke and night markets to hiking and cafés. Here’s what my average month looks like:

  • Movie ticket: ₩13,000–₩15,000
  • Café hopping weekends: ₩20,000–₩30,000
  • Drinks with friends: ₩30,000–₩60,000 (depends how much soju you can handle 🍶)

Compared to other big cities like Tokyo or New York, I find Seoul to be more budget-friendly when it comes to social life — you get quality experiences for a reasonable price.

💡 Final Thoughts — Is Korea Expensive in 2025?

Living in Korea in 2025 isn’t cheap, but it’s worth every won. I’d say an average single person in Seoul spends around ₩2.2–₩2.8 million/month (USD 1,600–2,000) for a comfortable lifestyle. If you live outside Seoul, your cost can drop by 30% easily.

From the clean streets and fast Wi-Fi to the amazing food and vibrant nightlife, Seoul offers one of the most dynamic urban experiences in the world. Whether you’re here to study, work, or explore, the cost of living is balanced by the quality of life and cultural richness Korea offers.

Would I recommend living here? Absolutely — just be ready for expensive coffee, small apartments, and unforgettable experiences.
item.excerpt:encoded: Curious about the real cost of living in Korea in 2025? Here’s my honest review after living in Seoul — from rent and food to entertainment and transport. Spoiler: it’s not cheap, but it’s worth it.

item.title: Where to Stay on Airbnb in Korea (2025): Best Seoul Areas and Legal Tips for Foreign Visitors
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
🏡 Finding the Perfect Airbnb in Korea 2025

If you’re planning a trip to Korea in 2025, Airbnb is one of the most popular accommodation options for foreign travelers — especially in Seoul. From palaces and street food markets to nightlife and shopping, the city has everything within easy reach. But before booking, it’s important to know which areas are best and what legal rules apply to Airbnb stays in Korea.

🗺️ Best Airbnb Locations in Seoul for Foreign Visitors

Foreign tourists tend to stay in central Seoul — where sightseeing, transportation, and cultural experiences are easily accessible. Here are the most recommended districts for your next trip:

🏯 Gyeongbokgung Area

Perfect for travelers who love Korean history. Many Airbnb hosts operate near Gyeongbokgung Palace, offering traditional hanok stays with modern touches. You’ll be steps away from famous spots like Bukchon Hanok Village and Insadong.

🛍️ Myeongdong & Sogong-dong

Myeongdong is one of the most foreigner-friendly areas in Seoul — surrounded by landmarks such as Namdaemun, Gyeongbokgung, Deoksugung, and Namsan Tower. Subway access is excellent, and most hosts offer English-speaking support. Great for shopping and first-time visitors.

🎨 Hongdae & Sinchon

If you want to experience Seoul’s youthful side, Hongdae and Sinchon are must-visits. Close to Hongik University, these areas are full of guesthouses, indie cafés, and nightlife. It’s a favorite among budget travelers and students.

🕍 Jongno & Dongdaemun

These districts blend Seoul’s history with local charm. You’ll be near Dongdaemun Market, Gwangjang Market, and UNESCO-listed palaces like Changdeokgung. Keep in mind that average Airbnb rates here tend to be higher due to location demand.

💎 Gangnam, Itaewon & Yongsan

For luxury seekers, Gangnam and Apgujeong offer upscale stays and high-end shopping. Meanwhile, Itaewon, Hannam, Haebangchon, and Yongsan are the heart of the foreign community — filled with international restaurants and multicultural vibes. About 62% of Seoul’s Airbnb listings are concentrated in Yongsan District.

🌊 Other Popular Cities for Airbnb in Korea

  • Jeju Island: Korea’s largest Airbnb market, offering scenic ocean views and strong income potential for hosts. The average monthly revenue per room is around ₩1.9 million, with over 50% occupancy.
  • Gangneung & Sokcho: Coastal cities just a few hours from Seoul. With over 3,000 listings, these destinations are perfect for summer vacations.
  • Busan: A vibrant seaside city with roughly 2,500 listings and a 37% occupancy rate — offering both city life and beaches.
  • Jeonju: Known for its Hanok Village, with about 1,000 Airbnb stays blending tradition and comfort.

🚨 Legal Requirements & Safety Tips for Airbnb Guests

While Airbnb is widely used in Korea, the country enforces strict laws under “Urban Guesthouse Business for Foreign Tourists”. If you’re a foreign visitor, here’s what you should know before booking:

  • Foreign-Only Stays: Only foreigners are legally allowed to stay in urban guesthouses registered under this system. Koreans cannot book these accommodations unless in special pilot regions (e.g., Gangneung, Jeonju).
  • Host Residency Required: The host must live in the property and offer basic cultural experiences or foreign-language support.
  • Building Restrictions: Only detached houses, multi-family homes, or small apartments under 230m² can be used. Officetels and studio rooms are not allowed.
  • Safety Standards: Fire alarms, extinguishers, evacuation maps, and emergency lights are required by law. Buildings over 3 stories must have escape equipment installed.
  • Registration Requirement: As of October 2024, Airbnb requires hosts to upload business registration certificates. Existing hosts must comply by October 16, 2025, or risk listing removal by 2026.

💬 Note: Guests are not penalized for staying at an unregistered property, but they may face difficulties if legal issues arise — such as cancellation disputes or safety concerns.

📊 Market Trends & Guest Satisfaction

As of March 2024, Seoul had around 10,687 active Airbnb listings, recovering to nearly 90% of pre-pandemic levels. Popular districts include Mapo, Jung, Jongno, and Gangnam. In 2022, Airbnb transactions in Seoul reached ₩150.7 billion across 590,000 bookings — showing a steady market rebound.

However, guests report that cleanliness, communication, and check-in experience can vary. Always read reviews carefully before booking.

💡 Final Thoughts

Staying in an Airbnb in Korea is one of the best ways to experience local culture and live like a Seoulite. Choose neighborhoods close to public transport, double-check legal compliance, and you’ll have a safe, authentic stay. Whether it’s a hanok near Gyeongbokgung or a rooftop apartment in Itaewon, Korea’s Airbnb scene in 2025 is more exciting and diverse than ever.

💬 Frequently Asked Questions

1. Can foreigners legally use Airbnb in Korea?

Yes, foreigners can use Airbnb in Korea through licensed “Urban Guesthouse for Foreign Tourists.” However, most are for foreign guests only, and hosts must live in the property.

2. Which area is best for Airbnb stays in Seoul?

For first-time visitors, Myeongdong and Hongdae are ideal due to their access to shopping, nightlife, and public transport. For culture lovers, Gyeongbokgung and Jongno offer traditional hanok experiences.

3. Are there restrictions on Airbnb for Koreans?

Yes. In most areas, Koreans cannot stay in foreign-only Airbnb accommodations. Only a few cities like Gangneung or Jeonju allow limited domestic stays under special permits.

4. What should I check before booking?

Check if the host is registered under “Urban Guesthouse for Foreign Tourists,” read recent reviews, and confirm amenities such as heating, air conditioning, and fire safety devices.
item.excerpt:encoded: Planning to use Airbnb in Korea? Discover the best Seoul neighborhoods for foreign travelers, key legal rules, and tips for a safe and authentic stay in 2025 — from Myeongdong to Hongdae and Jeju.

item.title: Grocery Shopping in Korea 2025: Where to Buy Food, Essentials, and International Products
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
🛒 Grocery Shopping in Korea — My 2025 Guide

Living in Korea means getting used to a lot of new things — language, culture, and yes, grocery shopping! Whether you’re an expat staying long-term or a traveler looking to cook at your Airbnb, Korea offers plenty of options. From big supermarkets like E-Mart and Homeplus to online platforms like Coupang and Market Kurly, here’s everything you need to know about where and how to shop for food and essentials in Korea.

🏬 Major Supermarkets in Korea

Supermarkets in Korea are modern, clean, and well-organized — but they can vary a lot in price and product selection. Here’s a quick breakdown of the main ones you’ll encounter.

1️⃣ E-Mart (이마트)

E-Mart is Korea’s largest retail chain and the go-to for locals. It’s great for fresh produce, meat, snacks, and household items. Prices are moderate, and they often have weekly discounts. You’ll find imported goods like cheese, pasta, and cereal too.

💡 Tip: E-Mart Traders (이마트 트레이더스) is like a Korean Costco — perfect for bulk shopping.

2️⃣ Homeplus (홈플러스)

Homeplus is slightly cheaper than E-Mart and has a strong focus on convenience. Most branches are connected to subway stations or malls. It’s a good choice for ready-made meals and local snacks. Some stores even stay open late, making it great for busy schedules.

3️⃣ Lotte Mart (롯데마트)

Lotte Mart is popular among both Koreans and foreigners for its import section. You’ll find Western sauces, spices, and cereals not available elsewhere. There’s also a tax-free counter for international travelers — perfect if you’re near Seoul Station or Jamsil.

📦 Online Grocery Platforms

In 2025, most Koreans buy groceries online. Delivery is fast, reliable, and often arrives within a few hours — even at midnight!

Coupang (쿠팡)

The Amazon of Korea! Coupang’s “Rocket Delivery” service delivers overnight or same-day to most cities. You can order everything — food, toiletries, electronics, and even international snacks. Prices are usually lower than offline stores.

💬 Personal tip: I order bottled water, cleaning supplies, and imported sauces from Coupang — they arrive by 7 a.m. the next day!

Market Kurly (마켓컬리)

Market Kurly is a premium online grocery store focusing on fresh, organic, and gourmet food. It’s pricier than Coupang but offers high-quality meats, seafood, and produce. The “Morning Delivery” service delivers by 7 a.m. — perfect for busy Seoulites.

SSG.com (신세계몰)

Operated by Shinsegae, SSG.com connects with E-Mart and Starbucks, letting you order your favorite snacks and daily essentials in one place. It’s ideal for expats who want convenience and quality in one app.

🌍 Where to Find International Groceries

Sometimes you just crave home — a jar of peanut butter, a can of chickpeas, or that one hot sauce you miss. Thankfully, Seoul has some amazing international grocery stores.

Itaewon & Yongsan

These neighborhoods are foreigner hubs, full of international supermarkets and import stores. You’ll find ingredients from the U.S., Europe, India, and Southeast Asia. Popular stores include:

  • Foreign Food Mart (이태원): Carries Middle Eastern and South Asian ingredients — spices, lentils, basmati rice, and halal products.
  • High Street Market (한남동): Western-style supermarket with cheeses, pasta, sauces, and baking goods.
  • Kim’s Mart (용산): Offers imported snacks, condiments, and drinks from various countries.

Gangnam & Apgujeong

Upscale areas like Gangnam and Apgujeong also feature smaller specialty markets selling imported wine, organic food, and high-end products for international residents.

💸 Grocery Prices in 2025: My Honest Review

Prices have gone up since 2023, but groceries in Korea are still fairly reasonable depending on your lifestyle. Here’s what I usually pay as of 2025:

  • 🍚 Rice (10kg): ₩30,000–₩40,000
  • 🥬 Kimchi (2kg): ₩15,000–₩25,000
  • 🥩 Beef (1kg): ₩35,000–₩60,000
  • 🍗 Chicken (1kg): ₩10,000–₩15,000
  • 🥛 Milk (1L): ₩2,500–₩3,000
  • ☕ Imported coffee beans (250g): ₩12,000–₩18,000

💬 My tip: Korean produce (like cabbage, tofu, eggs) is affordable, but imported snacks and meats can be pricey. Mix both to balance your grocery budget.

💡 Final Thoughts — Grocery Shopping Made Easy

Shopping for groceries in Korea is now easier than ever. You can combine traditional markets, modern supermarkets, and online delivery for a comfortable and affordable lifestyle. Whether you’re browsing E-Mart aisles or ordering from Coupang at midnight, Korea’s grocery system is designed for convenience. Once you learn where to find what you need, grocery shopping becomes one of the most enjoyable parts of expat life here.

💬 Frequently Asked Questions

1. What’s the best supermarket for foreigners in Korea?

E-Mart and Lotte Mart are great for everyday shopping, while Itaewon’s Foreign Food Mart and High Street Market are best for international ingredients.

2. Is grocery delivery common in Korea?

Yes! Most Koreans use Coupang or Market Kurly for next-day delivery. It’s reliable, fast, and supports English-friendly apps.

3. Where can I buy international food in Seoul?

Head to Itaewon, Yongsan, or Hannam for foreign grocery stores. You’ll find imported sauces, spices, and Western snacks easily.

4. Are groceries expensive in Korea?

It depends. Local items are affordable, but imported goods can be pricey. A monthly grocery budget of ₩300,000–₩500,000 is typical for one person.
item.excerpt:encoded: Wondering where to buy food and essentials in Korea? From E-Mart and Homeplus to Coupang and Market Kurly, here’s your 2025 guide to grocery shopping in Seoul — plus where to find international ingredients.

item.title: Best Day Trips from Seoul (2025 Updated Guide)
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
🚆 Explore Beyond Seoul — 2025 Day Trip Guide

Seoul is an incredible city, but sometimes you just need a break from the busy streets and neon lights. Luckily, Korea’s amazing transportation system makes day trips from Seoul quick, easy, and affordable. Whether you want nature, culture, or seaside vibes, here’s my updated list of the best 2025 day trips from Seoul — all tested and loved!

🏞️ 1. Nami Island (남이섬)

Probably the most famous day trip from Seoul — and for good reason! Nami Island is a peaceful half-moon-shaped island known for its tree-lined paths, riverside cafés, and romantic vibes. It’s gorgeous in every season, from snowy winters to golden autumns.

  • How to get there: ITX train from Yongsan Station to Gapyeong Station (≈1 hour), then take the ferry to the island.
  • Don’t miss: Nami zipline, tree tunnel walk, and lakeside brunch cafés.
  • Travel tip: Combine with Petite France and Garden of Morning Calm nearby!

🌸 2. Suwon (수원)

Just an hour south of Seoul, Suwon is a UNESCO World Heritage city famous for Hwaseong Fortress. Walk the castle walls, visit traditional markets, and enjoy delicious “Suwon galbi” (marinated ribs). It’s the perfect blend of history and food.

  • How to get there: Subway Line 1 to Suwon Station (≈1 hour).
  • Don’t miss: Hwaseong Fortress, Suwon Fried Chicken Street, and Haenggung Palace.

🌄 3. Pocheon Art Valley (포천아트밸리)

If you’re looking for something more offbeat, Pocheon Art Valley is a former quarry turned into a stunning cultural park. The turquoise lake surrounded by cliffs is breathtaking — perfect for photos and a peaceful stroll.

  • How to get there: Take the subway to Uijeongbu Station, then a local bus to Art Valley (≈2 hours total).
  • Don’t miss: Sculpture park, monorail ride, and glass observatory.

🌊 4. Incheon Chinatown & Songdo

Just west of Seoul, Incheon offers a mix of history, culture, and seaside breeze. Start at Chinatown, eat Jajangmyeon (black bean noodles) where it originated, then head to Songdo for modern architecture and waterfront parks.

  • How to get there: Subway Line 1 to Incheon Station (≈1 hour 10 mins).
  • Don’t miss: Chinatown murals, Jayu Park, Songdo Central Park, and the Tri-Bowl.
  • Bonus: Try a sunset walk over Songdo’s G-Tower Skywalk.

🏖️ 5. Gangneung (강릉)

If you crave the ocean, take a morning KTX train to Gangneung — beachside cafés, pine forests, and seafood await. It’s one of my favorite escapes from Seoul, especially for coffee lovers.

  • How to get there: KTX from Seoul Station to Gangneung Station (≈2 hours).
  • Don’t miss: Anmok Coffee Street, Gyeongpo Beach, and Jumunjin BTS Bus Stop.
  • Pro tip: Go early, rent an e-bike, and return on the evening train.

🌿 6. Garden of Morning Calm (아침고요수목원)

Located in Gapyeong, this botanical garden is pure serenity. It’s famous for its seasonal flower festivals and winter lighting display. Ideal for families, couples, or anyone needing a break from the city.

  • How to get there: Take the ITX to Cheongpyeong, then a local bus or taxi (≈90 mins).
  • Don’t miss: Lighting Festival (Nov–Mar) and tulip blooms in spring.

🏕️ 7. Chuncheon (춘천)

Another popular trip in Gangwon Province — Chuncheon is known for lakes, cable cars, and the famous dakgalbi (spicy stir-fried chicken). It’s quiet, green, and perfect for a one-day getaway.

  • How to get there: ITX from Yongsan Station to Chuncheon (≈1 hour 10 mins).
  • Don’t miss: Soyang River Skywalk, Legoland Korea, and Dakgalbi Street.

💡 Final Thoughts — Easy Escapes from Seoul

Korea’s high-speed trains and well-connected subway lines make weekend escapes from Seoul incredibly easy. Whether you love beaches, mountains, art, or palaces, there’s a day trip for everyone. My top tip? Leave early, plan meals ahead, and enjoy the journey — every corner outside Seoul offers something beautifully different.

💬 Frequently Asked Questions

1. What are the easiest day trips from Seoul by train?

Nami Island, Suwon, and Incheon are the most convenient — all within 1 hour from Seoul by subway or ITX train.

2. Can I visit Nami Island and Garden of Morning Calm on the same day?

Yes! They’re both located in Gapyeong and easily connected by shuttle bus. Start early to enjoy both comfortably.

3. What’s the best seaside day trip from Seoul?

Gangneung and Incheon’s Songdo area are top choices for ocean views, cafés, and seafood — both reachable by KTX or subway.

4. Are these day trips foreigner-friendly?

Absolutely. Most destinations have English signs, tourist shuttles, and easy public transport from Seoul. Apps like Naver Map and KakaoT make navigation simple.
item.excerpt:encoded: Discover the best day trips from Seoul in 2025 — from Nami Island and Suwon Fortress to Gangneung’s beaches and Incheon Chinatown. Perfect for travelers who want a quick escape from the city.

item.title: Hidden Gems in Korea Beyond Seoul (Underrated Travel Spots)
item.dc:creator: duja0518
item.content:encoded:
🌄 Discover Korea’s Hidden Treasures Beyond Seoul

Seoul may steal the spotlight, but some of Korea’s most unforgettable experiences lie outside the capital. If you’ve already visited Namsan Tower and Myeongdong, it’s time to explore deeper — into the underrated destinations that locals love but tourists often overlook. Here’s my 2025 guide to the best hidden gems in Korea for those who crave authenticity, nature, and calm.

🏔️ 1. Jecheon (제천) — The Healing City of Lakes

Known as “the city of healing,” Jecheon is surrounded by mountains and lakes. It’s a favorite weekend escape for Koreans who want peace and nature without crowds. The highlight is Cheongpung Lake, where you can ride a cable car with jaw-dropping views of emerald water and forested cliffs.
  • How to get there: KTX from Seoul to Jecheon Station (≈1.5 hours).
  • Don’t miss: Cheongpung Lake cable car, Uirimji Reservoir, and local herbal markets.
  • Why go: Perfect mix of mountain air and water views — ideal for a wellness trip.

🌿 2. Tongyeong (통영) — Korea’s Coastal Gem

Nicknamed the “Naples of Korea,” Tongyeong is a charming port city filled with art, seafood, and sea views. The colorful murals at Dongpirang Village and the Hallyeosudo Cable Car make it one of the most scenic towns on the southern coast.
  • How to get there: Express bus from Seoul (≈4.5 hours) or train to Jinju, then local bus.
  • Don’t miss: Tongyeong Cable Car, Luge Ride, and seafood markets near Gangguan Port.
  • Travel vibe: Romantic seaside strolls, fresh oysters, and sunset views.

🌸 3. Hadong (하동) — Tea Fields & Slow Travel

Hadong is a hidden paradise for tea lovers. Its green tea terraces stretch across misty valleys along the Seomjin River. Unlike crowded Boseong, Hadong remains quiet and authentic. You can walk through tea fields, taste hand-picked teas, and even take part in traditional tea ceremonies.
  • How to get there: KTX to Jinju → local bus to Hadong (≈3 hours total).
  • Don’t miss: Hwagae Market, Ssanggyesa Temple, and the Green Tea Cultural Center.
  • Best season: Late spring (April–May) when the tea fields are lush and fragrant.

🌅 4. Mokpo (목포) — History Meets Ocean Views

Mokpo offers a beautiful mix of colonial history, mountain hiking, and seaside cafes. The city’s Gatbawi Rock and Yudalsan Mountain provide sweeping coastal views, while its art museums and seafood restaurants keep things interesting.
  • How to get there: KTX from Seoul to Mokpo Station (≈2.5 hours).
  • Don’t miss: Mokpo Modern History Museum, Yudalsan Skywalk, and seafood stew by the harbor.
  • Insider tip: Visit nearby Sinan Islands for a real off-grid adventure.

🌊 5. Ulleungdo (울릉도) — Korea’s Mysterious Island

If you want a true hidden gem, Ulleungdo is your destination. Located in the East Sea, this volcanic island feels untouched — full of cliffs, sea caves, and crystal-clear waters. Locals call it “the island that guards time.”
  • How to get there: Bus or train to Gangneung → ferry to Ulleungdo (≈6–7 hours total).
  • Don’t miss: Haengnam Coastal Trail, Nari Basin, and local squid dishes.
  • Best time to visit: May to September — calm seas and clear skies.

🌻 6. Jeonju Hanok Village (전주 한옥마을)

While Jeonju is becoming more popular, it’s still a must-visit for culture lovers. The Hanok Village offers over 700 traditional houses where you can rent hanbok, try bibimbap, and enjoy makgeolli at cozy taverns. The slower pace here feels worlds away from Seoul.
  • How to get there: KTX from Yongsan to Jeonju (≈1.5 hours).
